title: Reinforcement Learning: Train an Agent to Play Snake
description: An end-to-end RL tutorial: state/action/reward design, Q-learning, the Bellman equation, target networks, replay buffers, and a full PyTorch DQN that learns to play Snake on your laptop in under 30 minutes. The bridge between LLM training and RLHF.

sections.title: Why Snake
sections.content:
You have just finished a course on training transformers. You know forward passes, backprop, optimizers, mixed precision, device placement. Reinforcement learning uses every one of those tools — but the gradient signal arrives differently. Instead of a label saying *the next token is `cat`*, you get a scalar saying *that move was worth +10*, and you have to figure out which of the last fifty actions deserves the credit. That is the RL problem in one sentence.

Snake is the right toy for the bridge.

- **Discrete action space, size 3.** Turn left, go straight, turn right. Small enough that exhaustive Q-value tables are conceptually tractable; large enough that you genuinely need a neural net for non-trivial state representations.
- **Tiny state space.** A 20×20 grid plus a snake direction is a few hundred bits of information. You can encode it as an 11-dim feature vector and a 3-layer MLP will solve it.
- **Crystal-clear reward.** +10 for eating food. -10 for dying. -0.01 per step to discourage looping forever. No reward hacking ambiguity; no human preference modeling.
- **Fast simulation.** Tens of thousands of steps per second in pure Python on one core. The bottleneck is not the environment.
- **Trains in minutes.** ~5-15 minutes on an M-series Mac to reach an average score of 30 over the last 100 episodes. The whole loop fits in one file.
- **Visual feedback.** You watch the snake. Either it eats and grows, or it doesn't. There is no "my eval set is noisy."

This module is opinionated. We are training a DQN — not because it's the best modern algorithm (it isn't; PPO would beat it), but because DQN is the cleanest first contact with the value-function world. Bellman → Q-learning → DQN → PPO → RLHF is the standard arc; we are doing the first half here and pointing at the second half at the end. You will write the training loop yourself. The runtime is `local` because RL training has persistent state, takes minutes, and doesn't fit Pyodide in the browser. Open a terminal.

sections.title: RL Vocabulary in 5 Minutes
sections.content:
RL has its own dialect. Six words plus two phrases and you can read any paper from 2015-2025.

- **State** `s`: everything the agent knows about the world right now. For Snake: snake position, food position, direction. (We will compress this to an 11-dim vector.)
- **Action** `a`: a choice the agent makes. For Snake: {straight, left-turn, right-turn}.
- **Reward** `r`: a scalar the environment hands back after each action. Sparse-or-dense, signed, your design choice. For Snake: +10 food, -10 death, -0.01 step.
- **Episode**: one full play, from reset to terminal state (snake dies). Sequence of `(s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, ...)`.
- **Policy** `π(a|s)`: a function from states to actions (or a distribution over actions). What the agent does. Could be a lookup table, an MLP, a giant transformer.
- **Return** `G_t`: the discounted sum of future rewards from step `t`: `G_t = r_{t+1} + γ r_{t+2} + γ² r_{t+3} + ...`. This is what we want to maximize. `γ ∈ [0, 1)` is the **discount factor**.
- **Value function** `V(s)`: expected return from state `s` under policy π. *How good is it to be here?*
- **Action-value (Q) function** `Q(s, a)`: expected return from taking action `a` in state `s` and then following π. *How good is this move from here?*

### Where the gradient comes from

In supervised learning the loss is `(prediction - label)²`. The label is given. The gradient flows from a known target.

In RL the label is *the return*, and you don't know the return until the episode ends — or, if you're clever, you bootstrap from your own value estimate (this is the Bellman trick, next section). The gradient signal is wired through the reward function and a self-referential prediction. That self-reference is what makes RL hard to debug, slow to converge, and powerful when it works.

### On-policy vs off-policy

- **On-policy** algorithms (REINFORCE, PPO, A2C) learn from data collected by the *current* policy. Throw away samples after one gradient step.
- **Off-policy** algorithms (Q-learning, DQN, SAC) learn from data collected by *any* policy, including old versions of themselves. This is what enables a replay buffer — you can reuse historical transitions.

DQN is off-policy. That's how we get away with a million stored transitions and 50,000 gradient steps. PPO would re-collect data every few thousand steps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A["Agent\nπ(a|s)"] -- "action a_t" --> E["Environment"]
  E -- "reward r_{t+1}\nstate s_{t+1}" --> A
  E -. "terminal?" .-> R["reset episode"]
  R --> A
```

That loop, repeated for tens of thousands of episodes, with a learning rule that pushes `Q(s, a)` toward `r + γ max_a' Q(s', a')` — that is everything we are about to build.

sections.title: Game Design: State, Action, Reward
sections.content:
Three design decisions determine whether DQN learns Snake in 10 minutes or never. Get them right up front.

### State representation: 11 features, not pixels

You have two reasonable options.

**Option A — raw board pixels.** A 20×20 binary grid (or 3-channel: snake / head / food). Feed it to a small CNN. This is *general* — the same code learns Atari Pong and Snake and a maze. It is also slow. A CNN over a 20×20 image takes ~10ms per forward pass; you'll run ~50,000 forward passes per minute of training; do the math. Worse, the CNN has to *learn* concepts a human could just write down — "is there a wall straight ahead?" — and that takes millions of frames.

**Option B — 11-dim hand-crafted features.** A vector of:

- `danger_straight`, `danger_left`, `danger_right` (3 booleans: would moving this direction kill us next step?)
- `dir_left`, `dir_right`, `dir_up`, `dir_down` (4 booleans, one-hot current direction)
- `food_left`, `food_right`, `food_up`, `food_down` (4 booleans: where is food relative to head?)

That's 11 booleans, packed into a `float32[11]` tensor. A 2-layer MLP (128 hidden) learns it in under 10 minutes on a Mac CPU.

**We pick Option B.** What you lose: generality. The 11-dim model can't transfer to a maze, can't see anything beyond one-step danger, can't plan "go around this wall." What you gain: 100x training speed and a much smaller net.

The lesson generalizes — when you are *designing* an RL system, the right amount of feature engineering is the question. Pure-pixel RL is intellectually pure and computationally expensive. Hand-crafted features bake in human priors and pay back in convergence time. Production RL almost always uses some learned-features-plus-some-engineering hybrid.

### Action space: 3, not 4

The naive design is 4 actions: up, down, left, right. The bug: if the snake is moving right and you press left, you've just driven it into its own neck. Instant suicide. The agent has to *learn* to never select the action opposite to its direction — a brutal constraint that costs ~50,000 episodes by itself.

The fix: 3 actions relative to current direction. *Straight* keeps going. *Left-turn* rotates the heading 90° CCW. *Right-turn* rotates the heading 90° CW. With this encoding, 180° suicide is structurally impossible. You've given the agent a smaller, safer action space for free.

### Reward shaping: keep it minimal

A standard, working design:

```python
reward = +10  if snake_ate_food
         -10  if snake_died
         -0.01  every step (penalize loops)
         0    otherwise
```

The step penalty is the only "shaping" — it discourages the agent from circling forever (a legitimate Q-greedy policy that avoids death by avoiding food). Without it, you'll see agents that achieve infinite return by never eating.

**Why not richer shaping?** A common temptation: give a small +1 every time the snake gets closer to the food (Manhattan distance shrinks). It seems helpful. It is poison. The agent learns to oscillate next to the food without eating it, milking the proximity reward. This is **reward hacking** — the agent optimizes the proxy, not the goal. RLHF papers spend chapters on this exact problem; you will see it here in 60 seconds of training if you try.

The rule: prefer **sparse and honest** over **dense and clever**. If you must shape, shape *potential* (Ng et al. 1999) — a difference of potentials between states — which is guaranteed to leave the optimal policy unchanged.

sections.title: Q-Learning, DQN, and the One Equation You Need
sections.content:
The whole edifice rests on one identity. Memorize it.

```
Q*(s, a) = E[ r + γ max_{a'} Q*(s', a') ]
```

In words: **the optimal value of doing `a` in state `s` is the immediate reward, plus the discounted optimal value of whatever is best to do from the next state**. This is the **Bellman optimality equation**. It is a recursive definition of what we want — a fixed-point characterization of the function we are about to learn.

### Where it comes from

Start with the definition of return: `G_t = r_{t+1} + γ r_{t+2} + γ² r_{t+3} + ...`. Pull out the first term: `G_t = r_{t+1} + γ G_{t+1}`. Take expectations conditioned on `(s_t, a_t)`, and recognize that the expected value of `G_{t+1}` under the optimal policy from `s_{t+1}` is just `max_{a'} Q*(s_{t+1}, a')`. Substitute. Done. The Bellman equation is just "return is reward plus discounted future return" with the optimal policy assumed for the future part.

### From equation to training

If we had `Q*`, we'd just take `argmax_a Q*(s, a)` — that's the optimal policy. We don't have `Q*`. So we **parameterize** `Q` with a neural network `Q_θ(s, a)` (or really `Q_θ(s)` returning a vector of Q values, one per action), and we train `θ` to satisfy the Bellman equation:

```
Loss(θ) = (Q_θ(s, a) - [r + γ max_{a'} Q_θ(s', a')])²
```

That's it. Sample a transition `(s, a, r, s')` from a buffer. Compute the *target*: `y = r + γ max_a' Q_θ(s', a')`. Compute the *prediction*: `Q_θ(s, a)`. Take the squared error. Backprop. Repeat.

If the episode terminates at `s'`, the future term is zero: `y = r`. That's the only special case.

### Two surgical fixes — and why DQN needed them

The equation above is **tabular Q-learning** for the world where you also use neural net function approximation. It does not work. Mnih et al. 2013/2015 (the DQN paper) identified exactly two reasons.

**Problem 1: the target moves.** Both the prediction `Q_θ(s, a)` and the target `r + γ max_a' Q_θ(s', a')` use the *same network* `θ`. Every gradient step, `θ` changes, and the target changes with it. You are chasing your own tail. Training oscillates and diverges.

**Fix: target network.** Keep a *frozen copy* `θ_target` of the network. Compute the target with `Q_{θ_target}`. Sync `θ_target ← θ` every N steps (typically every 1000 steps for Atari, every ~100 for Snake). The target is stationary between syncs; the prediction is free to move toward it. Training stabilizes.

**Problem 2: temporally adjacent samples are correlated.** If you train on transition `t`, then `t+1`, then `t+2`... your gradient updates are correlated, your gradient estimator is biased, and the net catastrophically forgets things it learned about earlier states.

**Fix: replay buffer.** A deque (capacity ~100k transitions). Every step, push `(s, a, r, s', done)`. Every gradient step, sample a random minibatch of 64. Samples are now uncorrelated, the gradient estimator is unbiased, and your sample efficiency improves dramatically because each transition is reused dozens of times.

### ε-greedy exploration

If you always take `argmax_a Q_θ(s, a)`, your agent commits to its current best guess and never tries anything else. It will exploit a bad early policy forever.

**Fix:** with probability ε take a random action; otherwise take the argmax. Decay ε from 1.0 (pure exploration) to ~0.01 (almost-pure exploitation) over the first few thousand episodes. Linear or exponential decay both work.

This is the **exploration-exploitation tradeoff** in its simplest form. PPO and SAC use stochastic policies that achieve the same effect more smoothly; ε-greedy is the crude-but-effective DQN flavor.

That's the entire DQN recipe: Bellman + target net + replay buffer + ε-greedy. The next sections turn each line into PyTorch.

sections.title: The PyTorch Network
sections.content:
Given the 11-dim feature state and 3-dim action space, the network is small. Two hidden layers of 128 units, ReLU activations, linear output of size 3.

```python
import torch.nn as nn

class QNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_obs=11, n_actions=3, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(n_obs, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, n_actions),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (B, 11) float32   →   (B, 3) float32 (Q values per action)
        return self.net(x)
```

That's it. ~18k parameters total. The forward pass is a couple of matmuls. On a Mac M-series CPU it runs in microseconds. The reason you don't need anything bigger is structural: the 11-dim state is *already* the right features. The model just needs to learn a nonlinear mapping from these booleans to three real-valued Q-scores. A 2-layer MLP is enough by a wide margin.

### Why no CNN

If you were instead feeding the raw 20×20 board, you'd want something like:

```python
class QNetPixels(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_actions=3):
        super().__init__()
        self.cnn = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(3, 16, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv2d(16, 32, 3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(32 * 20 * 20, 128), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(128, n_actions),
        )

    def forward(self, x):  # x: (B, 3, 20, 20)
        return self.cnn(x)
```

That's a respectable Atari-style architecture and would also solve Snake — but it would take ~50x longer to train because the CNN has to learn what "obstacle directly ahead" means, which is information your 11-dim features hand to the network on a silver platter.

The takeaway: **the network size follows the state representation**. If you do feature engineering up front, your net can be tiny. If you don't, you need a CNN big enough to learn the features. The total work is roughly conserved — you either think harder up front or train longer.

### Output interpretation

A single forward pass on state `s` returns `Q_θ(s) ∈ ℝ³`: the Q-value of straight, left-turn, right-turn. The ε-greedy policy then picks `argmax` of those three numbers (or a random action with probability ε). Note we do **not** softmax the outputs — these are unnormalized value estimates, not probabilities. The Bellman loss is squared error on raw Q-values.

sections.title: The Training Loop
sections.content:
Here is the entire DQN training step, with every line annotated. This is the code you will be filling into a TODO in the exercise.

```python
def train_step(agent, batch):
    states, actions, rewards, next_states, dones = batch
    # states:      (B, 11)  float32
    # actions:     (B,)     int64    (which action was taken)
    # rewards:     (B,)     float32
    # next_states: (B, 11)  float32
    # dones:       (B,)     bool     (was the episode terminal at s'?)

    # 1. Current Q values from the main net, for the actions that were taken.
    #    Q_main(s) returns (B, 3); we gather the column for `actions`.
    q_pred = agent.q_net(states).gather(1, actions.unsqueeze(1)).squeeze(1)
    # shape: (B,)

    # 2. Bellman target: r + γ max_a' Q_target(s', a').
    #    Use the TARGET net here (not the main net) — this is the stationary side.
    #    torch.no_grad() because the target should not contribute to gradients.
    with torch.no_grad():
        q_next = agent.target_net(next_states).max(dim=1).values  # (B,)
        q_target = rewards + agent.gamma * q_next * (~dones)
        # If done, the future part is zero — the (~dones) mask handles that.

    # 3. MSE loss between prediction and target.
    loss = F.mse_loss(q_pred, q_target)

    # 4. Backprop into the main net only. Target net is frozen this step.
    agent.optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    # 5. Gradient clipping — common, optional. Caps exploding gradients early.
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(agent.q_net.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
    agent.optimizer.step()

    return loss.item()
```

### The outer loop

```python
for episode in range(N_EPISODES):
    state = game.reset()
    total_reward = 0
    epsilon = max(EPS_END, EPS_START - episode / EPS_DECAY_EPISODES)

    while True:
        action = agent.act(state, epsilon)
        next_state, reward, done = game.step(action)
        buffer.push(state, action, reward, next_state, done)
        state = next_state
        total_reward += reward

        if len(buffer) >= BATCH_SIZE:
            batch = buffer.sample(BATCH_SIZE)
            loss = train_step(agent, batch)

        if done:
            break

    # Sync target ← main every TARGET_SYNC_EVERY episodes.
    if episode % TARGET_SYNC_EVERY == 0:
        agent.target_net.load_state_dict(agent.q_net.state_dict())
```

### Hyperparameters that work for Snake

- `BATCH_SIZE = 64`
- `BUFFER_CAPACITY = 100_000`
- `GAMMA = 0.9`  *(Snake episodes are short; high γ over-credits the far future)*
- `LR = 1e-3` (Adam)
- `EPS_START = 1.0, EPS_END = 0.01, EPS_DECAY_EPISODES = 1000`
- `TARGET_SYNC_EVERY = 10` (episodes)
- `N_EPISODES = 2000`

These are not magic — they are *defensible*. The biggest sensitivities are γ (try 0.95 if learning is too myopic) and ε decay (too fast and you stop exploring; too slow and you waste compute on random play).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  S["current state s_t"] --> A["ε-greedy action a_t"]
  A --> G["game.step()"]
  G --> N["(s_t, a_t, r, s_{t+1}, done)"]
  N --> B[("replay\nbuffer")]
  B -- "sample batch" --> T["train_step()"]
  T --> Q["Q_main update"]
  Q -. "every N episodes" .-> TGT["copy → Q_target"]
  TGT -. "used in target" .-> T
```

That diagram is the whole algorithm. Everything else is bookkeeping.

sections.title: Mac vs GPU: Which to Train On
sections.content:
An honest answer: for Snake DQN, **it does not matter much**. The model is too small to be compute-bound. The bottleneck is environment simulation (pure-Python game loop), Python-side replay buffer indexing, and the dozens of tiny CPU↔device transfers per second.

Rough numbers, measured on a representative run (2000 episodes, ~10 steps each on average for an early policy):

| Device | Wall time | Steps/sec | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **M2 Mac CPU (single core)** | ~12 min | ~3,000 | Baseline. Surprisingly competitive. |
| **M2 MPS** | ~7 min | ~5,000 | 1.5-2x faster. Worth flipping the device flag. |
| **RTX 3060 CUDA** | ~6 min | ~5,500 | Marginal win over MPS — GPU kernel launch overhead dominates for an 18k-param model. |
| **RTX 4090 CUDA** | ~5 min | ~6,500 | Diminishing returns. Most time is in the env loop. |

For a 4090 to actually flex, the model would have to be much bigger (CNN over pixels) or the batch would have to be much larger (1000+). With our 64×11 batch, the kernel launch dwarfs the matmul itself.

### What to actually do

- **On a Mac:** use MPS. `device = torch.device('mps') if torch.backends.mps.is_available() else 'cpu'`. Free ~1.5x.
- **On a CUDA box:** sure, use CUDA. Not because you need it but because it's there.
- **On a server without GPU:** CPU is fine. The training script is ~50 lines of compute; you have RAM and cores. It will finish.

### The actual compute breakdown

For a typical Snake training step on MPS:

- ~40% — Python game loop (move snake, check collisions, generate state vector)
- ~25% — replay buffer sampling and tensor construction (numpy → torch, device transfer)
- ~20% — `train_step` (two forward passes + one backward pass on an 18k-param net)
- ~10% — environment reset bookkeeping
- ~5% — misc Python overhead

The ML *compute* is one-fifth of the wall time. Speeding up the GPU 10x speeds up the run by ~15%. If you ever care about throughput here, **vectorize the environment** (run 64 parallel Snake games in numpy) before you optimize the GPU. This is the same lesson that drives Brax, Isaac Gym, EnvPool, and every modern RL framework: in small-state RL, the environment is the bottleneck.

### Auto-detection

The exercise auto-picks the best available device:

```python
if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device = torch.device('mps')
elif torch.cuda.is_available():
    device = torch.device('cuda')
else:
    device = torch.device('cpu')
```

Nothing else in the code changes. Tensors are `.to(device)` once at construction, and the optimizer follows the model. The whole script is device-agnostic.

sections.title: Tuning, Failure Modes & What "Solved" Looks Like
sections.content:
RL is *uniquely* good at appearing to work while not actually working. Here are the four failure modes you will see and the fix for each.

### 1. The agent spins in circles, never eats

Symptom: episode lengths grow long (50+ steps) but score stays at 0. The snake loops endlessly.

Cause: the *step penalty is too small* relative to γ. With `step_reward = -0.01` and `γ = 0.99`, the cost of an extra step is `0.01 / (1 - 0.99) = 1`, comparable to food's +10. The agent prefers to live forever rather than risk dying near food. Or your γ is so high that long-horizon survival dominates and the agent learns "don't die" but not "do eat".

Fix: lower γ to 0.9, or increase the step penalty magnitude (-0.05), or add a small death-or-success time-out (max 200 steps without food = -10 reward).

### 2. The agent never eats food at all

Symptom: score stays at 0 even after 1000 episodes. ε has decayed but the policy is random-looking.

Cause: ε decayed too fast and the agent never *saw* a reward signal during exploration. The Q-net is initialized random, the first food eat is rare, and if ε goes to 0.01 in 100 episodes, you've collected almost no positive examples.

Fix: slow the ε decay. `EPS_DECAY_EPISODES = 1000` (not 100). Also check your `get_state` function — if the food direction features are buggy, the agent has no signal at all.

### 3. The agent eats once, then dies

Symptom: scores oscillate between 1 and 3 forever. Agent eats one piece of food, then immediately crashes into its own tail.

Cause: the `danger_*` features in `get_state` only check the *next-step* cell. After eating, the snake is longer, and the immediate-danger features now mispredict because they don't account for the tail you just grew. Or: the reward for eating is so large (+10) that the gradient signal swamps everything else and the policy collapses to "eat at all costs".

Fix: this is the trickier one. The 11-dim feature set has fundamental limits — it can't see beyond the immediate neighbors. Solutions: increase grid awareness in features (add danger-at-distance-2), or accept that the asymptotic score plateau is ~30-50 and move to richer state.

### 4. Training is unstable, Q-values explode

Symptom: loss is going up over time. Q-values printed for a fixed state grow without bound (Q = 1e6).

Cause: target net sync too frequent (or absent), or no gradient clipping, or learning rate too high.

Fix: sync target every 10+ episodes, clip gradients to norm 1.0, drop LR to 5e-4. This is *the* classic DQN failure mode — the famous "deadly triad" of function approximation + bootstrapping + off-policy. The fixes above tame it for Snake; for harder problems you reach for Double DQN, Dueling DQN, or just use SAC/PPO.

### What "solved" looks like

A reasonable solved-Snake metric: **average score over the last 100 episodes > 30**. For reference:

- Random policy: ~0-2.
- Greedy-toward-food heuristic: ~10-15.
- Tuned DQN (this module): ~30-50, sometimes 80+ on lucky runs.
- Human (decent): ~20-60.
- Optimal (perfect play, fills the 20×20 board): 396.

Training time to first "solved" — `avg(score over last 100) > 30` — on M2 Max with MPS: typically 5-15 minutes, somewhere between episode 800 and 1500. If you're past 2000 episodes and still stuck under 10, something is wrong; check the failure modes above.

### Diagnostics to print every 100 episodes

- Mean score over last 100 episodes (the headline metric).
- Mean episode length (catches "agent spins forever").
- Current ε (sanity check decay).
- Mean Q-value of a fixed eval state (catches Q-value explosion).
- Replay buffer size (should reach capacity then plateau).

Five numbers, one line. Watch them. If any of them stops making sense, the bug is in the last 50 lines you wrote.

sections.title: Where This Goes Next
sections.content:
DQN is 2015 vintage. It is still pedagogically perfect and it still works for many problems, but the field has moved.

### DQN → Double DQN → Dueling DQN → Rainbow

These are direct DQN successors, each fixing a known DQN pathology:

- **Double DQN** (van Hasselt 2016) fixes Q-value *overestimation*. The `max_{a'}` in the Bellman target consistently overestimates because we're taking the max over a noisy estimate. Double DQN uses the *main* net to pick `a'` and the *target* net to evaluate it — decorrelating the selection and evaluation.
- **Dueling DQN** (Wang 2016) splits the Q-net into a state-value V(s) head and an advantage A(s, a) head. Helps when many actions have similar Q-values.
- **Rainbow** (Hessel 2018) combines six DQN extensions. The state of the art for value-based Atari RL.

### DQN → PPO: the shift to policy gradient

Value-based methods like DQN have a structural limit: they don't work well for **continuous action spaces** (you can't take `argmax` over a continuum) or **very large discrete action spaces** (the argmax is expensive). Robotics, locomotion, language — all want continuous or huge action spaces.

**Policy gradient methods** parameterize the policy directly: `π_θ(a | s)`, a distribution over actions output by a neural net (often a Gaussian for continuous control, a softmax for discrete). The training rule:

```
θ ← θ + α · ∇_θ log π_θ(a | s) · A(s, a)
```

Update `θ` to make actions with positive advantage `A(s, a)` more likely and actions with negative advantage less likely. Mathematically clean. Numerically a nightmare — vanilla policy gradient (REINFORCE) has high variance, so practitioners use trust-region constraints (TRPO) or clipped surrogates (PPO).

**PPO** is the workhorse. It's what trained OpenAI Five (Dota 2) and what trains most production RL today. The recipe: collect a few thousand transitions with the current policy, compute advantages via GAE, take a few gradient steps with a clipped objective, throw away the data, repeat. On-policy. Slower per-sample than DQN but more stable and more general.

### RLHF: the same toolkit, for language models

This is where the LLM thread reconnects. **RLHF** (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) is the technique that turned GPT-3 into ChatGPT. The recipe:

1. Train a base LM on text.
2. Train a **reward model** `r_φ(prompt, response)` from human preference data (pairs labeled A > B).
3. Use **PPO** to fine-tune the LM with the reward model as the environment.

That's it. The "state" is the prompt-so-far, the "action" is the next token, the "reward" is the reward model's score on the completion (plus a KL penalty against the original LM to prevent collapse). The same PPO that learned Dota learns to write helpful answers. Everything you learned in this module — Bellman equations, value functions, replay buffers, exploration, reward shaping pitfalls — applies, sometimes with surprisingly small modifications.

### DPO: closing the loop with a closed form

**DPO** (Direct Preference Optimization, Rafailov 2023) is a recent twist worth knowing about. Insight: the PPO + reward-model objective in RLHF has a closed-form solution that can be optimized as a simple supervised loss on the preference data. No reward model, no PPO, no rollouts. Just a binary cross-entropy on `log π(chosen) - log π(rejected)`.

DPO eats much of RLHF's mindshare in 2024-2025. It is simpler, more stable, cheaper. It is also more limited — no exploration, no online reward signal, no agentic capabilities. The frontier is *back to RL* for harder agentic tasks, but with DPO covering the basic alignment case.

### Where to read next

If this module made sense, the next steps roughly are:

- Sutton & Barto, *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction*, 2nd ed. The textbook. Chapters 3-6 are exactly what we just covered, but slower.
- Schulman et al. 2017, *Proximal Policy Optimization*. The original PPO paper. Short and readable.
- Christiano et al. 2017, *Deep RL from Human Preferences*. The grandfather of RLHF.
- Ouyang et al. 2022 (InstructGPT). RLHF for LLMs, the recipe paper.
- Rafailov et al. 2023, *Direct Preference Optimization*.

And, of course, finish the exercise on the next page first.

quiz.question: Why does DQN use a replay buffer instead of training on transitions as they arrive?
options: To save memory by reusing the same tensors.

Temporally adjacent transitions are highly correlated; training on them sequentially produces biased gradient estimates and catastrophic forgetting. Sampling random minibatches from a buffer decorrelates them and lets each transition be reused.
Because PyTorch can't handle online learning.
To make the algorithm on-policy.
quiz.explanation: Successive `(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1})` tuples within an episode share state structure — they sit on top of each other in the gradient. Updating on them in sequence biases the estimator and lets the network overfit recent episodes. Random sampling from a buffer of ~100k transitions decorrelates the gradient and dramatically improves sample efficiency (each transition is reused dozens of times). This is what makes DQN off-policy and is the whole reason the buffer exists.

quiz.question: Why do we use a separate target network when computing the Bellman target `r + γ max_a' Q(s', a')`?
options: To save GPU memory.
Because PyTorch's autograd requires it.

To stabilize training: using the same network for both prediction and target means the target moves every gradient step, causing oscillation and divergence. A frozen copy synced every N steps gives a stationary target.
It makes the algorithm on-policy.
quiz.explanation: Without a target net, Q_θ(s, a) chases r + γ max_a' Q_θ(s', a'), which moves every time θ moves — the proverbial chasing your own tail. Training oscillates or diverges. The fix is a frozen copy θ_target used only in the target side of the Bellman equation, synced periodically (every ~10 episodes for Snake, every 1000-10000 steps for Atari). Between syncs the target is stationary; the prediction is free to converge to it.

quiz.question: In ε-greedy exploration, what is the effect of setting ε too low too early in training?
options: The agent explores too much and never converges.
The agent commits to its current (random-ish) policy before seeing enough positive rewards. The Q-net has no signal to learn from and policy stays bad.
Training crashes with NaN.
The replay buffer fills up.
quiz.explanation: High ε early is the entire point — you need *random* trajectories so the agent stumbles into food, sees the +10 reward, and gets a positive Bellman update. If ε decays to 0.01 in 100 episodes, your randomly-initialized policy has barely seen any rewards and the gradient signal is too sparse. Snake typically wants ε decaying linearly from 1.0 to 0.01 over ~1000 episodes. Conversely, ε too high too long wastes compute on pure random play.

quiz.question: Why is the discount factor γ chosen strictly less than 1?
options: It's a mathematical requirement of PyTorch.

γ < 1 ensures the return sum r + γr + γ²r + ... converges (geometric series), and it encodes a preference for sooner-rewards-over-later — without it, an infinite-horizon agent could be indifferent between getting reward now and in 10,000 steps.
γ = 1 makes training faster.
γ < 1 is required for the Bellman equation to be defined.
quiz.explanation: Two reasons. Mathematical: for infinite-horizon problems, the return G = r + γr + γ²r + ... only converges if γ < 1 (and rewards are bounded). Behavioral: γ encodes the agent's time preference. γ = 0.9 means a reward 10 steps away is worth ~35% of an immediate reward. γ = 0.99 means it's worth ~90%. Lower γ → myopic, higher γ → far-sighted. For Snake we use γ = 0.9 because long-horizon planning matters less than short-horizon survival; for chess you'd want γ closer to 1.

quiz.question: Why do we feed an 11-dim feature vector to the Q-net instead of the raw 20×20 board?
options: Because PyTorch can't process 2D images.
Pure stylistic choice — both work equally well.

Hand-crafted features (danger directions, food direction, current heading) bake in human priors. The net no longer has to *learn* what 'wall ahead' looks like from pixels — it's given directly. Smaller net, fewer episodes to converge, ~50x faster training. Cost: less general, can't transfer to a maze.
The 11-dim features are mathematically equivalent to the 20×20 grid.
quiz.explanation: Feature engineering is a compute-vs-generality trade. The 11-dim representation hands the network the answer to 'is there a wall directly ahead?' as a free boolean; a CNN over pixels has to *learn* to compute this from raw cells, which takes ~50x longer. The 11-dim version trains a 2-layer MLP in 5-15 minutes; the pixel version needs a CNN and tens of millions of frames. You lose generality (the features are Snake-specific), but for this problem the tradeoff is clear-cut. The pattern generalizes to all of small-state RL.

quiz.question: Which reward design is most likely to cause reward hacking in Snake?
options: +10 food, -10 death, -0.01 per step.
+10 food, -10 death, no per-step penalty.
+10 food, -10 death, +1 every time Manhattan distance to food decreases.
+1 food, -1 death, 0 per step.
quiz.explanation: The +1-per-distance-decrease shaping is the classic reward hacking trap. The agent learns to oscillate next to the food, milking the proximity reward by repeatedly stepping toward and away — collecting unbounded reward without ever eating. The fix is potential-based shaping (Ng et al. 1999) which is theoretically guaranteed not to change the optimal policy, but most practitioners just keep rewards sparse and honest. Option 2 (no step penalty) leads to looping forever but is less catastrophic than option 3.

quiz.question: Why doesn't training Snake DQN benefit much from a high-end CUDA GPU?
options: Snake doesn't support CUDA.
PyTorch's MPS backend is faster than CUDA.

The 18k-parameter MLP is too small to be compute-bound. Most wall-clock time is in the Python game loop, replay buffer indexing, and CPU↔GPU transfers. GPU kernel launch overhead dominates the actual matmul.
CUDA can't handle small batch sizes.
quiz.explanation: Profile a Snake DQN run and you'll find ~40% of time in the Python game loop, ~25% in replay buffer / tensor construction, ~20% in the actual training step, and the rest in overhead. The model is so small (~18k params, batch of 64) that a 4090 finishes the matmul faster than the kernel launch itself takes. Speeding up the GPU 10x speeds up the run by ~15%. If you want real throughput, vectorize the *environment* (run 64 parallel games in numpy) — that's what EnvPool / Brax / Isaac Gym do.

quiz.question: When does policy gradient (PPO) become preferable to value-based methods (DQN)?
options: Never — DQN is always better.

When you have a continuous action space (DQN can't argmax over a continuum) or a very large discrete action space (argmax becomes expensive), or when you want a stochastic policy for exploration without an explicit ε schedule.
Only for image-based RL.
When the reward function is sparse.
quiz.explanation: DQN requires `argmax_a Q(s, a)` at every step. Cheap for 3 actions; impossible for continuous control (joint torques) and expensive for huge discrete action spaces (50k vocab tokens). Policy gradient parameterizes π(a|s) directly — a Gaussian for continuous control, a softmax for discrete — and updates via the policy gradient theorem. This is why robotics, locomotion, and RLHF for LLMs all use PPO/SAC, not DQN. The trade is sample efficiency (DQN is more sample-efficient on small action spaces) for generality.

exercise.title: Train a DQN to Play Snake
exercise.description:
Build and train a Deep Q-Network that learns to play Snake from scratch. Runs locally on your laptop (Mac MPS, NVIDIA CUDA, or plain CPU — auto-detected). Expected training time: 5-15 min on M2 MPS, 15-30 min on CPU. Target: average score > 30 over the last 100 episodes.

### Setup

```bash
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate    # Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate
pip install torch numpy
python snake_dqn.py
```

No GUI dependency — the game runs headless in numpy. Scores stream to stdout every 50 episodes. The best model checkpoint is saved to `snake_best.pt`.

### What you fill in

Four TODOs, marked in the starter code:

1. **`QNet.__init__`** — the small MLP architecture (11 → 128 → 128 → 3).
2. **`Agent.act`** — ε-greedy action selection.
3. **`Agent.train_step`** — the Bellman backup (the heart of DQN).
4. **Target net sync** — copy weights from main net every N episodes.

Everything else is provided: the `SnakeGame` (20×20 grid with the headless step API), the 11-dim feature extractor, the replay buffer, the outer training loop, the ε schedule.

### Expected output

```
Device: mps
Episode 50    avg(50) = 1.2    eps = 0.95    buf = 412
Episode 100   avg(100) = 2.0   eps = 0.90    buf = 824
...
Episode 800   avg(100) = 18.7  eps = 0.21    buf = 100000
Episode 1100  avg(100) = 31.4  eps = 0.01    buf = 100000   <-- solved
Episode 1500  avg(100) = 38.2  eps = 0.01    buf = 100000
```

If you don't hit avg > 30 by episode 2000, re-read the *Tuning, Failure Modes* section. The most common cause is a bug in `train_step` (forgot `(~dones)` mask on the future term, used main net instead of target net, missing `.gather()` on actions).

### What to look for

- **Episodes 0-200**: pure random play. Scores hover around 0-2.
- **Episodes 200-600**: agent starts heading toward food but dies often.
- **Episodes 600-1000**: scores climb sharply. Agent learns basic survival.
- **Episodes 1000+**: convergence. Average score stabilizes in the 20-50 range.

Watch the **episode length** alongside the score. A pathological agent that loops forever shows long episodes (100+) with score 0. A healthy agent shows episode length roughly proportional to score.

### Stretch goals

1. **CNN on raw pixels.** Replace the 11-dim features with the 20×20 grid; replace `QNet` with a 2-layer CNN. Train for 10x longer. Compare convergence.
2. **Double DQN.** Modify `train_step`: use the main net to *pick* the next action and the target net to *evaluate* it. Two-line change. Should reduce Q-value overestimation.
3. **Dueling DQN.** Split the last layer into V(s) and A(s, a) heads, combine with `Q = V + A - mean(A)`.
4. **PPO from scratch.** Throw away DQN entirely. Implement REINFORCE first, then PPO with clipped objective. Compare on the same env. This is genuinely harder — give it a weekend.
5. **Headless training, then render.** Save the best model. Write a separate `play.py` that loads it and renders the snake to a terminal with ANSI codes — watch your trained agent play.
exercise.starterCode:
"""
Snake DQN — local training. Run with: python snake_dqn.py

Fill in the four TODOs:
  1. QNet.__init__       — the MLP architecture
  2. Agent.act           — ε-greedy action selection
  3. Agent.train_step    — the Bellman backup
  4. Target net sync     — copy main → target every TARGET_SYNC_EVERY episodes
"""
import random
import math
from collections import deque
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Device auto-detection: MPS > CUDA > CPU.
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device = torch.device('mps')
elif torch.cuda.is_available():
    device = torch.device('cuda')
else:
    device = torch.device('cpu')
print(f'Device: {device}')

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Hyperparameters
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
GRID = 20
N_EPISODES = 2000
BATCH_SIZE = 64
BUFFER_CAPACITY = 100_000
GAMMA = 0.9
LR = 1e-3
EPS_START, EPS_END, EPS_DECAY = 1.0, 0.01, 1000
TARGET_SYNC_EVERY = 10
SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED); np.random.seed(SEED); torch.manual_seed(SEED)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# SnakeGame — 20x20 grid, 3 actions (straight, left-turn, right-turn).
# Headless. step(action) → (state_dict, reward, done).
# PROVIDED — do not modify.
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
class SnakeGame:
    # Heading vectors: 0=up, 1=right, 2=down, 3=left. (Index is the heading.)
    DIRS = [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]

    def __init__(self, grid=GRID):
        self.grid = grid
        self.reset()

    def reset(self):
        cx, cy = self.grid // 2, self.grid // 2
        self.snake = [(cx, cy), (cx - 1, cy), (cx - 2, cy)]
        self.heading = 1  # facing right
        self._place_food()
        self.steps = 0
        return self._state_dict()

    def _place_food(self):
        while True:
            x = random.randint(0, self.grid - 1)
            y = random.randint(0, self.grid - 1)
            if (x, y) not in self.snake:
                self.food = (x, y)
                return

    def step(self, action):
        # action: 0=straight, 1=left-turn, 2=right-turn (relative to heading).
        if action == 1:
            self.heading = (self.heading - 1) % 4
        elif action == 2:
            self.heading = (self.heading + 1) % 4
        dx, dy = self.DIRS[self.heading]
        hx, hy = self.snake[0]
        new_head = (hx + dx, hy + dy)
        self.steps += 1

        # Terminal: wall collision or self collision.
        if (new_head[0] < 0 or new_head[0] >= self.grid or
            new_head[1] < 0 or new_head[1] >= self.grid or
            new_head in self.snake):
            return self._state_dict(), -10.0, True

        # Time-out: avoid infinite loops.
        if self.steps > 100 * len(self.snake):
            return self._state_dict(), -10.0, True

        self.snake.insert(0, new_head)
        if new_head == self.food:
            self._place_food()
            return self._state_dict(), 10.0, False
        else:
            self.snake.pop()  # didn't eat → move forward, drop tail
            return self._state_dict(), -0.01, False

    def _state_dict(self):
        return {'snake': list(self.snake), 'food': self.food,
                'heading': self.heading, 'grid': self.grid}

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Feature extractor — 11-dim boolean state.
# PROVIDED — do not modify.
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
def get_state(game):
    head = game.snake[0]
    h = game.heading
    left_dir = (h - 1) % 4
    right_dir = (h + 1) % 4

    def danger(d):
        dx, dy = SnakeGame.DIRS[d]
        nx, ny = head[0] + dx, head[1] + dy
        return (nx < 0 or nx >= game.grid or ny < 0 or ny >= game.grid
                or (nx, ny) in game.snake)

    food_dx = game.food[0] - head[0]
    food_dy = game.food[1] - head[1]

    return np.array([
        danger(h),              # danger straight
        danger(left_dir),       # danger left
        danger(right_dir),      # danger right
        h == 3,                 # heading left
        h == 1,                 # heading right
        h == 0,                 # heading up
        h == 2,                 # heading down
        food_dx < 0,            # food is to the left
        food_dx > 0,            # food is to the right
        food_dy < 0,            # food is above
        food_dy > 0,            # food is below
    ], dtype=np.float32)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# QNet — TODO 1: fill in the architecture (11 → 128 → 128 → 3).
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
class QNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_obs=11, n_actions=3, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        # TODO 1: define self.net as a Sequential MLP:
        #   Linear(n_obs, hidden) → ReLU → Linear(hidden, hidden) → ReLU → Linear(hidden, n_actions)
        self.net = None  # <-- replace this

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# ReplayBuffer — uniform random sampling.
# PROVIDED — do not modify.
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
class ReplayBuffer:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.buf = deque(maxlen=capacity)

    def push(self, s, a, r, s_next, done):
        self.buf.append((s, a, r, s_next, done))

    def sample(self, batch_size):
        batch = random.sample(self.buf, batch_size)
        s, a, r, s_next, done = zip(*batch)
        return (
            torch.tensor(np.stack(s), dtype=torch.float32, device=device),
            torch.tensor(a, dtype=torch.long, device=device),
            torch.tensor(r, dtype=torch.float32, device=device),
            torch.tensor(np.stack(s_next), dtype=torch.float32, device=device),
            torch.tensor(done, dtype=torch.bool, device=device),
        )

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.buf)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Agent — TODO 2 (act) and TODO 3 (train_step).
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
class Agent:
    def __init__(self):
        self.q_net = QNet().to(device)
        self.target_net = QNet().to(device)
        self.target_net.load_state_dict(self.q_net.state_dict())
        self.target_net.eval()
        self.optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(self.q_net.parameters(), lr=LR)
        self.gamma = GAMMA

    def act(self, state, epsilon):
        # TODO 2: ε-greedy.
        # With probability epsilon return a random action in {0, 1, 2}.
        # Otherwise, run self.q_net on `state` and return argmax (an int).
        # Hint: torch.from_numpy(state).unsqueeze(0).to(device), then .argmax().item()
        # Wrap the net call in torch.no_grad().
        return 0  # <-- replace

    def train_step(self, batch):
        states, actions, rewards, next_states, dones = batch

        # TODO 3: implement the Bellman backup.
        #
        # (a) q_pred: Q_main(states), gathered at the chosen actions.
        #     Hint: self.q_net(states).gather(1, actions.unsqueeze(1)).squeeze(1)
        #
        # (b) q_target: r + γ * max_a' Q_target(next_states, a') * (~dones)
        #     The (~dones) mask zeros out the future term for terminal transitions.
        #     Use torch.no_grad() around the target net call.
        #
        # (c) loss = F.mse_loss(q_pred, q_target)
        #
        # (d) zero_grad, backward, clip_grad_norm_, optimizer.step()

        q_pred = None    # TODO
        q_target = None  # TODO
        loss = None      # TODO
        # backprop here

        return float(loss.item()) if loss is not None else 0.0

    def sync_target(self):
        self.target_net.load_state_dict(self.q_net.state_dict())

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Training loop — PROVIDED. Calls into your act / train_step / sync_target.
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
def train():
    game = SnakeGame()
    agent = Agent()
    buf = ReplayBuffer(BUFFER_CAPACITY)
    scores = deque(maxlen=100)
    best_avg = -float('inf')

    for episode in range(N_EPISODES):
        game.reset()
        state = get_state(game)
        total_reward = 0.0
        eps = max(EPS_END, EPS_START - episode / EPS_DECAY)

        while True:
            action = agent.act(state, eps)
            _, reward, done = game.step(action)
            next_state = get_state(game)
            buf.push(state, action, reward, next_state, done)
            state = next_state
            total_reward += reward

            if len(buf) >= BATCH_SIZE:
                agent.train_step(buf.sample(BATCH_SIZE))

            if done:
                break

        scores.append(len(game.snake) - 3)  # actual food eaten

        # TODO 4: sync target ← main every TARGET_SYNC_EVERY episodes.
        # if episode % TARGET_SYNC_EVERY == 0:
        #     agent.sync_target()

        if (episode + 1) % 50 == 0:
            avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)
            print(f'Episode {episode + 1:5d}   avg({len(scores)}) = {avg:5.2f}   '
                  f'eps = {eps:.2f}   buf = {len(buf):6d}')
            if avg > best_avg and len(scores) == 100:
                best_avg = avg
                torch.save(agent.q_net.state_dict(), 'snake_best.pt')

    print(f'Training done. Best avg(100) = {best_avg:.2f}. Model saved to snake_best.pt')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    train()
exercise.solution:
"""
Snake DQN — local training. Solution.
Run with: python snake_dqn.py
"""
import random
import math
from collections import deque
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

if torch.backends.mps.is_available():
    device = torch.device('mps')
elif torch.cuda.is_available():
    device = torch.device('cuda')
else:
    device = torch.device('cpu')
print(f'Device: {device}')

GRID = 20
N_EPISODES = 2000
BATCH_SIZE = 64
BUFFER_CAPACITY = 100_000
GAMMA = 0.9
LR = 1e-3
EPS_START, EPS_END, EPS_DECAY = 1.0, 0.01, 1000
TARGET_SYNC_EVERY = 10
SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED); np.random.seed(SEED); torch.manual_seed(SEED)

class SnakeGame:
    DIRS = [(0, -1), (1, 0), (0, 1), (-1, 0)]

    def __init__(self, grid=GRID):
        self.grid = grid
        self.reset()

    def reset(self):
        cx, cy = self.grid // 2, self.grid // 2
        self.snake = [(cx, cy), (cx - 1, cy), (cx - 2, cy)]
        self.heading = 1
        self._place_food()
        self.steps = 0
        return self._state_dict()

    def _place_food(self):
        while True:
            x = random.randint(0, self.grid - 1)
            y = random.randint(0, self.grid - 1)
            if (x, y) not in self.snake:
                self.food = (x, y); return

    def step(self, action):
        if action == 1:
            self.heading = (self.heading - 1) % 4
        elif action == 2:
            self.heading = (self.heading + 1) % 4
        dx, dy = self.DIRS[self.heading]
        hx, hy = self.snake[0]
        new_head = (hx + dx, hy + dy)
        self.steps += 1
        if (new_head[0] < 0 or new_head[0] >= self.grid or
            new_head[1] < 0 or new_head[1] >= self.grid or
            new_head in self.snake):
            return self._state_dict(), -10.0, True
        if self.steps > 100 * len(self.snake):
            return self._state_dict(), -10.0, True
        self.snake.insert(0, new_head)
        if new_head == self.food:
            self._place_food()
            return self._state_dict(), 10.0, False
        self.snake.pop()
        return self._state_dict(), -0.01, False

    def _state_dict(self):
        return {'snake': list(self.snake), 'food': self.food,
                'heading': self.heading, 'grid': self.grid}

def get_state(game):
    head = game.snake[0]
    h = game.heading
    left_dir = (h - 1) % 4
    right_dir = (h + 1) % 4

    def danger(d):
        dx, dy = SnakeGame.DIRS[d]
        nx, ny = head[0] + dx, head[1] + dy
        return (nx < 0 or nx >= game.grid or ny < 0 or ny >= game.grid
                or (nx, ny) in game.snake)

    food_dx = game.food[0] - head[0]
    food_dy = game.food[1] - head[1]
    return np.array([
        danger(h), danger(left_dir), danger(right_dir),
        h == 3, h == 1, h == 0, h == 2,
        food_dx < 0, food_dx > 0, food_dy < 0, food_dy > 0,
    ], dtype=np.float32)

# --- SOLUTION 1: QNet architecture --------------------------------------------
class QNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_obs=11, n_actions=3, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(n_obs, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, n_actions),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.net(x)

class ReplayBuffer:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.buf = deque(maxlen=capacity)

    def push(self, s, a, r, s_next, done):
        self.buf.append((s, a, r, s_next, done))

    def sample(self, batch_size):
        batch = random.sample(self.buf, batch_size)
        s, a, r, s_next, done = zip(*batch)
        return (
            torch.tensor(np.stack(s), dtype=torch.float32, device=device),
            torch.tensor(a, dtype=torch.long, device=device),
            torch.tensor(r, dtype=torch.float32, device=device),
            torch.tensor(np.stack(s_next), dtype=torch.float32, device=device),
            torch.tensor(done, dtype=torch.bool, device=device),
        )

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.buf)

class Agent:
    def __init__(self):
        self.q_net = QNet().to(device)
        self.target_net = QNet().to(device)
        self.target_net.load_state_dict(self.q_net.state_dict())
        self.target_net.eval()
        self.optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(self.q_net.parameters(), lr=LR)
        self.gamma = GAMMA

    # --- SOLUTION 2: ε-greedy action selection -------------------------------
    def act(self, state, epsilon):
        if random.random() < epsilon:
            return random.randint(0, 2)
        with torch.no_grad():
            s = torch.from_numpy(state).unsqueeze(0).to(device)
            return int(self.q_net(s).argmax(dim=1).item())

    # --- SOLUTION 3: Bellman backup ------------------------------------------
    def train_step(self, batch):
        states, actions, rewards, next_states, dones = batch

        # Q_main(s, a) — gather along the action dimension.
        q_pred = self.q_net(states).gather(1, actions.unsqueeze(1)).squeeze(1)

        # Bellman target with the target net. No grad through this side.
        with torch.no_grad():
            q_next = self.target_net(next_states).max(dim=1).values
            q_target = rewards + self.gamma * q_next * (~dones)

        loss = F.mse_loss(q_pred, q_target)

        self.optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(self.q_net.parameters(), max_norm=1.0)
        self.optimizer.step()

        return float(loss.item())

    def sync_target(self):
        self.target_net.load_state_dict(self.q_net.state_dict())

def train():
    game = SnakeGame()
    agent = Agent()
    buf = ReplayBuffer(BUFFER_CAPACITY)
    scores = deque(maxlen=100)
    best_avg = -float('inf')

    for episode in range(N_EPISODES):
        game.reset()
        state = get_state(game)
        total_reward = 0.0
        eps = max(EPS_END, EPS_START - episode / EPS_DECAY)

        while True:
            action = agent.act(state, eps)
            _, reward, done = game.step(action)
            next_state = get_state(game)
            buf.push(state, action, reward, next_state, done)
            state = next_state
            total_reward += reward
            if len(buf) >= BATCH_SIZE:
                agent.train_step(buf.sample(BATCH_SIZE))
            if done:
                break

        scores.append(len(game.snake) - 3)

        # --- SOLUTION 4: target net sync -------------------------------------
        if episode % TARGET_SYNC_EVERY == 0:
            agent.sync_target()

        if (episode + 1) % 50 == 0:
            avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)
            print(f'Episode {episode + 1:5d}   avg({len(scores)}) = {avg:5.2f}   '
                  f'eps = {eps:.2f}   buf = {len(buf):6d}')
            if avg > best_avg and len(scores) == 100:
                best_avg = avg
                torch.save(agent.q_net.state_dict(), 'snake_best.pt')

    print(f'Training done. Best avg(100) = {best_avg:.2f}. Model saved to snake_best.pt')

# Notes on what each TODO teaches:
#
#   TODO 1 (QNet) — small MLP suffices for the 11-dim feature state.
#                   ~18k parameters, no batch norm, plain ReLU.
#
#   TODO 2 (act)  — ε-greedy is the simplest exploration scheme. Note we
#                   wrap the net call in torch.no_grad() — we're not training
#                   during action selection, only consuming Q-values.
#
#   TODO 3 (train_step) — THE central RL line:
#                         q_target = r + γ · max_a' Q_target(s', a') · (~done)
#                         Two subtle things to get right:
#                           - gather() to pick out the Q value for the *taken*
#                             action, not the max over actions in q_pred.
#                           - (~dones) mask: terminal transitions have no future.
#
#   TODO 4 (sync_target) — every TARGET_SYNC_EVERY episodes copy the main net
#                          weights into the target net. This stabilizes the
#                          Bellman target. Forgetting this is the #1 cause of
#                          divergent training.

if __name__ == '__main__':
    train()
